name: Pico Veleta — ascent to the top of Sierra Nevada
name_original: Pico Veleta — subida a la cima de Sierra Nevada
region_intro: Hiking around Cenes De La Vega offers routes through varied terrain, characterized by the Genil River valley and surrounding hills. The area provides views towards Granada and the Sacromonte district, with trails often traversing agricultural land and Mediterranean scrub. Elevation changes are common, ranging from gentle riverside paths to more sustained climbs on higher ground. Best hiking trails around Cenes De La Vega The most popular hiking route is Pico de la Cruz loop from Huétor Vega, a 6.4 miles (10.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views from a local hill. Another top favourite among local hikers is View of Sacromonte loop from Cenes de la Vega, a moderate 7.5 miles (12.0 km) path. This trail provides panoramic views of the historic Sacromonte area. Local hikers also love the Genil River in Pinos Genil loop from Cenes de la Vega, a 3.3 miles (5.3 km) trail leading through the Genil River valley, often completed in about 1 hour 24 minutes. Hiking around Cenes De La Vega is defined by river valleys, hills, and viewpoints overlooking Granada. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside strolls to moderate ascents. The routes in Cenes De La Vega are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 3,800 reviews. More than 19,500 hikers have used komoot to explore Cenes De La Vega's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Cenes De La Vega?
A: There are over 500 hiking trails around Cenes De La Vega. These routes cater to various skill levels, with nearly 200 easy trails, over 250 moderate options, and more than 70 challenging routes for experienced hikers.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hikes in Cenes De La Vega?
A: Yes, Cenes De La Vega offers several easy routes suitable for families. A good option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31822243" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Genil River in Pinos Genil loop from Cenes de la Vega&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.3 miles (5.3 km) long and follows the Genil River valley. Another easy trail is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31588546" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from Cerro del Sol loop from Cenes de la Vega&lt;/a&gt;, offering pleasant views over 3.9 miles (6.3 km).

Q: What kind of views can I expect on hikes around Cenes De La Vega?
A: Hiking routes in Cenes De La Vega often provide diverse views. You can expect panoramic vistas of the historic Sacromonte area, as seen on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39544277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Sacromonte loop from Cenes de la Vega&lt;/a&gt;. Many trails also offer views towards Granada and traverse the scenic Genil River valley and surrounding hills.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, many trails around Cenes De La Vega are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31580696" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de la Cruz loop from Huétor Vega&lt;/a&gt;, which offers local hill views, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19357578" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Genil River in Pinos Genil – Fountain of the Four Lions loop from Cenes de la Vega&lt;/a&gt;, a longer moderate option.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or landmarks to see near the trails?
A: The region boasts several interesting natural and historical sites. You can explore the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/141744" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Cahorros Gorge, Monachil&lt;/a&gt;, known for its hanging bridges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5309193" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hanging Bridge of Los Cahorros&lt;/a&gt;. Other highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3525967" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quéntar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6468950" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carmen de los Mártires Gardens&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Cenes De La Vega?
A: The best time for hiking in Cenes De La Vega is generally during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is typically mild and pleasant, making for comfortable conditions on the trails. Summers can be very hot, and winters, while generally mild, can bring cooler temperatures and occasional rain.

Q: Are there any advanced or challenging hiking routes in Cenes De La Vega?
A: Yes, for more experienced hikers, Cenes De La Vega offers over 70 difficult routes. These trails typically feature more significant elevation changes and longer distances, providing a greater challenge. You can find detailed information and GPX tracks for these routes on komoot to plan your adventure.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Cenes De La Vega?
A: The hiking trails in Cenes De La Vega are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 3,800 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, from gentle riverside paths to more sustained climbs, and the rewarding views towards Granada and the Sacromonte district.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trailheads in Cenes De La Vega?
A: Cenes De La Vega is well-connected to Granada by local bus services, which can provide access to the town itself. From there, many trailheads are accessible on foot. For specific routes, it's advisable to check local bus schedules and route maps to plan your approach to the starting points.

Q: Are there options for parking near the hiking trails?
A: Yes, Cenes De La Vega, being a residential area and a gateway to the surrounding hills, typically offers various parking options. You can often find street parking in and around the town, especially near popular access points for trails. It's always recommended to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to eat near the hiking trails?
A: Cenes De La Vega has a selection of local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can refuel before or after your hike. These establishments are generally located within the town center, making it convenient to grab a coffee, a snack, or a meal. Some trails may pass closer to these amenities than others, so check your route beforehand.

Q: Are there any gorges or canyons to explore in the area?
A: Yes, the region around Cenes De La Vega is known for its impressive gorges. The most famous is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/141744" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Cahorros Gorge, Monachil&lt;/a&gt;, which features stunning rock formations and the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5309193" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hanging Bridge of Los Cahorros&lt;/a&gt;. Another notable natural feature is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/432856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de las Palomas&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: This is about midway to the Veleta summit if you started from downtown Granada. The views at 1900 meters elevation are already breathtaking, though the thinner air and exercise might have something to do with that as well ;-)

From La Hoya de la Mora there is a barrier to prevent motor vehicles from entering. By bicycle you can continue going up.
To reach the top it is advisable to do it from May, since the accumulated snow of winter can make it impossible to access.
Its summit is at 3,400m and the asphalt ends at 3,300m from the top. The last km is done on a difficult path for a road bike.

An alternative road that avoids the Alto de Hazallanas, per la estreta vall de El Charcón. La carretera surt de la població de Güejar Sierra, passant per Maitena i que ressegueix el río Genil. A posteriori, s'enfila cap a la carretera de las Sabinas fins a Pradollano.
Güéjar Sierra is a picturesque Granada town at the foot of the Sierra Nevada, which preserves the charm of its narrow streets and whitewashed houses. Surrounded by mountains, viewpoints, and trails.
